// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 6 — THE HUMAN GATE

Fluency is not accuracy. A polished Claude output — clean prose, tidy table, confident chart — is not itself evidence th

NARRATION

Fluency is not accuracy. A polished Claude output , clean prose, tidy table, confident chart , is not itself evidence that the output is correct. The human gate is a specific discipline: you decide what evidence would confirm each claim, then you check that evidence before acting. Verification is not one thing. A citation needs source-opening. A number needs recalculation. A figure needs data and axis review. A workflow action needs permission and log review.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING SCENE

OPENING SCENE

Imagine a research analyst who asks Claude to summarize five recent papers on remote work productivity. The output arriv

Opening Scene — Three days later, reviewing the deck before a board meeting, she clicks one of the citations to pull up the paper. It do
NARRATION

Imagine a research analyst who asks Claude to summarize five recent papers on remote work productivity. The output arrives in four minutes: three tight paragraphs, five citations with journal names and years, clean synthesis, professional hedging. She pastes it into the background section of a presentation. Three days later, reviewing the deck before a board meeting, she clicks one of the citations to pull up the paper. It does not exist. Not a wrong year or a wrong journal , it does not exist.

// SLIDE 03 — THE CORE CONCEPT: WHAT THE GAT

THE CORE CONCEPT: WHAT THE GATE ACTUALLY IS

·Proofreading. Checking grammar and flow tells you whether the output is readable, not whether it is true.
·Admiring the format. A clean table can contain wrong denominators. A beautiful chart can mislead through axis choice. A
·Asking Claude to check itself. Asking "is this correct?" can help surface some issues, but it is not independent verific
NARRATION

"Human gate" names the boundary between Claude's execution and your judgment. Claude produces artifacts: summaries, tables, charts, code changes, drafted emails, structured plans, workflow actions. Your job is to decide whether each artifact is correct, safe, appropriate, and worth acting on. Microsoft Research, in a 2025 study of knowledge workers using generative AI, found that AI use shifts critical thinking from production toward verification, integration, and task stewardship (Microsoft Research 2025). The work does not disappear , it moves.

// SLIDE 04 — WHY FLUENCY IS NOT A VERIFICAT

WHY FLUENCY IS NOT A VERIFICATION SIGNAL.

Generative AI models produce text that reads as confident and coherent even when the content is wrong, unsupported, or s

Why Fluency Is Not a Verification Signal — Farquhar et al. (Nature, 2024) demonstrated this technically by measuring semantic entropy — the spread of meaning acros
NARRATION

Generative AI models produce text that reads as confident and coherent even when the content is wrong, unsupported, or self-contradicting. This is not a quirk to be fixed in the next model version , it is structural. A model trained to produce human-like text will produce text that sounds human-confident regardless of whether the underlying content is grounded. Farquhar et al.

// SLIDE 05 — THE VERIFICATION MATRIX

THE VERIFICATION MATRIX.

Summary — Unsupported or reversed claims
Citation — Fabricated or irrelevant source
Number or table — Wrong denominator, formula error, rounding
Chart or figure — Misleading encoding, overclaiming caption
NARRATION

Output type determines what kind of verification you need. The table below gives you the starting check for each common Claude output type. Sources: Research design framework drawn from arXiv 2403.04696; visual mislead typology from CHI 2023 (Misleading Beyond Visual Tricks); chart perception from Cleveland and McGill (1984).

// SLIDE 06 — WORKED EXAMPLE: AUDITING A LIT

WORKED EXAMPLE: AUDITING A LITERATURE SUMMARY

What Claude returns:→Step 1: Split into claims.→Step 2: Assign evidence type.→Step 3: Check a sample.→Step 4: Mark each claim.
NARRATION

Here is the verification loop in practice. A policy analyst asks Claude to summarize three government reports on housing affordability. What Claude returns: Three paragraphs, one per report, each with a direct figure (percentage of income spent on rent, number of units needed, projected shortfall by year). Smooth, confident, well-organized.

// SLIDE 07 — EDITING VERSUS AUDITING

EDITING VERSUS AUDITING.

This distinction matters enough to state plainly.

Editing Versus Auditing — Editing asks: Is the output clear, appropriately framed, well-organized, and suitable for the audience?
NARRATION

This distinction matters enough to state plainly. Editing asks: Is the output clear, appropriately framed, well-organized, and suitable for the audience?

// SLIDE 08 — RISK-TIERED GATING

RISK-TIERED GATING

·Skim the output for obvious errors.
·Does the overall structure match your intent?
·Are there any claims that would embarrass you if wrong?
·Take ten seconds, then proceed.
·Apply the verification matrix for each output type present.
NARRATION

Not every output requires the same depth of review. Applying strict audit to every informal brainstorm is impractical and counterproductive. A risk-tiered approach keeps verification proportional. Light gate , low-stakes, exploratory, easily reversible, no external audience: Skim the output for obvious errors. Does the overall structure match your intent? Are there any claims that would embarrass you if wrong? Take ten seconds, then proceed.

// SLIDE 09 — THE GATE IN EACH CLAUDE SURFAC

THE GATE IN EACH CLAUDE SURFACE.

In Claude chat:→In Claude Code:→In Claude Cowork:
NARRATION

The gate looks different depending on which Claude surface you are using: In Claude chat: You are gating claims and reasoning. The main verification moves are source checking, calculation spot-checking, and comparing the summary to the source document.

// SLIDE 10 — COMMON MISTAKES

COMMON MISTAKES

"I read it and it sounded right."→"Claude cited it, so it's real."→"The table looks clean."→"The chart is beautiful."→"A passing test means the code is complete."
NARRATION

"I read it and it sounded right." Fluency is not accuracy. Reading for sound and reading for truth are different skills. "Claude cited it, so it's real." Citations are text generation, not library lookups. Open the source.

// SLIDE 11 — THE ONE RULE

THE ONE RULE.

The research notes for this chapter propose one rule that survives every edge case:

The One Rule — No verification path, no delegation.
NARRATION

The research notes for this chapter propose one rule that survives every edge case: Before you ask Claude to do something, ask yourself: How will I know if the output is correct? What evidence would I check? What would I do if it were wrong? If you cannot answer those questions, the task is not ready to delegate. Either define the verification path first, or keep the task human-owned.
